subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, effective communication of the topic, situating Ronaldoâs transfer in both global football economics and Saudi Vision 2030
Precisely stated and well-focused research question that guides the entire discussion
Appropriate selection of a wide range of secondary sources and justification for exclusive reliance on them given contextual constraints
weaknesses: Methodology section remains largely descriptive and omits explicit criteria for source selection (dates, credibility checks)
Analytical tools and procedures (e.g., how Google Trends data were harvested and interpreted) are not fully specified
Title could be refined to more closely mirror the research question by explicitly naming Al Nassrâs acquisition of Cristiano Ronaldo
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate demonstrates very good focus and method overall. The topic is communicated accurately and comprehensively, and the research question is sharply defined and maintained. The methodology shows informed selection of secondary sources, although it lacks detail on analytical procedures and transparency in sampling criteria. These strengths place the work solidly in the 5â6 band, with only minor shortcomings preventing a perfect score.

strengths: Generally relevant and appropriate application of theory and data (e.g., Google Trends, jersey-sales figures) to the branding case
Correct and consistent use of business-management terminology (soft power, brand personality, growth-share matrix)
Clear and coherent organisation of subject knowledge, with accurate definitions contextualised to the Ronaldo case
weaknesses: Some secondary data (e.g., jersey-sales from sports blogs) are taken at face value without triangulation
Key terms such as âsoft powerâ are not defined or linked back to seminal theoretical sources
Assertions about Vision 2030 strategy are occasionally unsupported by primary policy documents
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work displays good subject knowledge and mostly appropriate use of terminology and source material. The literature is applied coherently, but reliance on some non-academic sources and occasional definitional gaps limit the depth of understanding. These considerations align with the 3â4 band.

strengths: Broad and relevant research base (25+ sources) directly tied to the research question
Quantitative analysis of follower counts, broadcast deals and revenue targets that moves beyond description
Well-developed argument linking Vision 2030 objectives, PIF strategy and brand-equity outcomes
weaknesses: Critical evaluation of alternative explanations (e.g., sports-washing critiques, framework limitations) is only briefly sketched
Analytical depth could be enhanced by further quantitative metrics (longitudinal attendance, financial returns)
Discussion of model constraints (e.g., Aakerâs framework when applied to an individual athlete) is underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidateâs critical thinking is strong overall, with good research relevance, clear analysis and a coherent, reasoned argument. The inclusion of revenue data and theoretical bridging demonstrates sophisticated handling of evidence. Some dimensions of critical evaluation and methodological critique remain under-explored, preventing an upper-range âexcellentâ rating but clearly justifying a high mark in the 10â12 band.

strengths: Structure fully complies with IB requirements: title page, contents, numbered sections, conclusion and bibliography
Consistent layout, clear headings, properly formatted citations and captions
Minor structural issues (e.g., abrupt conceptual transitions) do not impede overall readability or navigation
weaknesses: Occasional formatting inconsistencies in heading hierarchy and citation style
Long, complex sentences occasionally reduce clarity
One source is undated and lacks full bibliographic detail
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is largely impeccable, with all core elements correctly applied and a logical flow of sections. Minor formatting lapses and stylistic inconsistencies are present but do not hinder the reader. This performance aligns with the top band, meriting the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The research questionââHow did Al Nassrâs acquisition of Cristiano Ronaldo increase Saudi global visibility through soccer?ââis explicitly stated, narrow enough to be investigated in 4 000 words, and is consistently referenced across the literature review, analysis and conclusion. The student maintains focus on âvisibilityâ rather than drifting into peripheral financial or performance metrics.

While the question could have been sharpened further by defining specific visibility indicators at the outset, the link between the stated RQ and the ensuing discussion of media reach, social-media followers and broadcast deals is strong, demonstrating effective connection to the body of the essay.

The discussion develops a logical argument linking Vision 2030 goals, PIF strategy and brand-equity gains, and the conclusion synthesises findings coherently. The student acknowledges limitations such as potential media bias and the absence of primary data, showing some evaluative thinking.

However, critical evaluation is only partial: counter-arguments (e.g., sports-washing criticisms, sustainability of visibility) are briefly noted or omitted, and reference to theoretical limitations is minimal. Consequently, the argument is well-developed but not fully critical, fitting the 4-6 band at a score of 4.

The essay shows clear understanding of business-management theoriesâproduct life cycle, scenario planning, brand personality, brand familiarityâand applies them coherently to the Ronaldo case. Definitions are accurate and contextualised, indicating organised subject knowledge supported by sources.

However, depth is uneven: financial return on investment and stakeholder analysis are mentioned but not explored rigorously, and certain assertions about Saudi strategy lack substantiating data. The knowledge is therefore clear and coherent but falls short of the consistently insightful treatment characteristic of the top band.

Business-management terminologyâasset transfer, soft power, growth-share matrix, brand personality dimensionsâis used correctly and woven into the narrative. The student distinguishes between concepts such as brand familiarity and brand personality, applying them with mostly accurate nuance.

Minor lapses occur (e.g., interchange of âacquisitionâ and âtransfer feeâ without financial precision), but overall terminology is employed consistently and accurately, justifying placement in the 3-4 band.

The student analyses Instagram follower spikes, broadcast deals and Google Trend figures through frameworks like product life cycle and brand personality, moving beyond mere description. Conclusionsâfor example, that Ronaldo acted as a catalyst for subsequent player inflowsâare generally supported with data.

Yet the analysis often rests on single metrics without deeper quantitative evaluation (e.g., revenue impact, longitudinal attendance figures) and alternative explanations are seldom explored. The resulting depth is clear and largely supported, but not consistently rigorous, meriting the lower end of the 4-6 band.

Source materialâfrom peer-reviewed journals, Statista databases, reputable news outlets and corporate websitesâis largely relevant to branding theory and the Saudi Pro League case. The student integrates statistics (Instagram followers, Google Trends, broadcast territories) and aligns them with conceptual points, demonstrating mostly appropriate application.

Nonetheless, some evidence is taken at face value (e.g., jersey-sales reports from sports blogs) without triangulation, and quotations are occasionally used to illustrate rather than interrogate claims. This keeps the application at a solid but not exemplary level.

The methodology section justifies exclusive reliance on secondary data and explains why primary data were impractical given Saudi information constraints. A broad range of journalistic, academic and industry sources is listed, showing some informed selection.

However, the discussion of methods remains largely descriptive; it does not explain selection criteria (e.g., date range, credibility checks) nor does it outline specific analytical tools beyond general reference to frameworks. Consequently, the range is appropriate but evidence of an âeffective and informedâ selection process is partial rather than clear.

The bibliography lists over 25 sources spanning academic journals, industry reports and reputable news media, evidencing good breadth. Most sources relate directly to the RQ by addressing Saudi sports strategy, branding theory or Ronaldoâs market impact, demonstrating clear relevance.

Still, the heavy reliance on journalistic pieces and secondary statistics, coupled with the absence of primary data or triangulated academic studies, limits the research from reaching the âexcellentâ standard. The overall quality is therefore judged as solidly good.

Formatting broadly adheres to IB requirements: consistent font, clear sub-headings, page numbers, properly formatted table of contents and in-text citations. The bibliography follows a recognised citation style and images are captioned.

Some minor inconsistencies (e.g., occasional long URLs, slight over-reliance on in-text parenthetical notes rather than footnotes) are evident but do not impede readability or compliance. Overall, layout and formatting are correctly applied, earning a score in the top band.

The student presents a clear, business-oriented topicâSaudi Arabiaâs use of a high-profile sports acquisition to enhance national visibilityâand situates it within the broader context of global soccer economics and Vision 2030. Key concepts such as branding, market re-positioning and soft power are introduced early and elaborated throughout the introduction and literature review.

Explanations are generally comprehensive: background on the European dominance of club football, the Saudi Pro Leagueâs objectives and the commercial logic behind player transfers are all articulated with relevant detail. Occasional phrasing issues and a slight mis-alignment between the generic title and the specific research focus prevent full excellence, but overall the topic is communicated accurately and effectively.

The essay follows the expected EE structureâtitle page with word count, abstract omitted as per new guide, contents page, sequentially numbered sections, conclusion and bibliographyâindicating a clearly appropriate structure. Headings match the table of contents and guide the reader logically through the argument.

Minor issues exist (e.g., occasional redundancy between literature-review and analysis sections), yet all core structural elements are present and functional, justifying placement in the upper band, though not necessarily meriting the maximum score.

annotations.comment: The term âsoft powerâ is introduced without theoretical definition; define it and link it to relevant branding literature.
Word count is provided and falls within the 4 000-word limit, meeting IB formatting requirements.
Methodology section lacks detail on specific analytical frameworks and tools; specify how data will be systematically analyzed.
The diagram effectively illustrates Aakerâs five brand personality dimensions, enhancing theoretical clarity and visual engagement.
Analysis is largely descriptive; exploring counter-arguments (e.g., sports-washing criticisms) would strengthen critical depth.
The âSports Clubs Investment and Privatizationâ source is undated and lacks full bibliographic details; update citation accordingly.
Google Trends statistics are compelling, but specify search parameters, timeframe, and data collection methods to enhance transparency.
Effective use of Google Trends and jersey sales data demonstrates strong application of secondary source material in analysis.
The title is too generic â the student should refine it to explicitly reference Al Nassrâs acquisition of Cristiano Ronaldo to better align with the research question and focus of the study.
The research question is clearly and precisely stated, demonstrating strong focus and guiding the subsequent analysis effectively.
Table of contents is comprehensive and accurately reflects the report structure, aiding navigation and coherence.
Framing Cristiano Ronaldoâs transfer within Vision 2030 demonstrates a strong, focused connection to the research question and context.
Incorporation of specific revenue targets ($120 M to $480 M) adds robust quantitative support and enhances analytical credibility.
The jersey sales statistic relies on a single journalistic source; triangulate with official club or merchandising reports to verify accuracy.
Discussion successfully bridges brand familiarity theory with empirical observations, showcasing depth in analytical reasoning.
Section clearly outlines PIFâs strategic acquisitions, reinforcing the argument of market rebranding through ownership changes.
Introduction effectively contextualizes the global soccer market and establishes clear relevance to branding, revenue streams, and the studyâs focus.
In-text citation for (Capron, Dussauge, and Mitchell 1998) deviates from IB style; ensure citations follow a consistent authorâdate or footnote format.
Transition into âProduct life cycleâ is abrupt; add a linking sentence to improve logical flow between conceptual sections.
Expand analysis by explaining how PIF ownership translates into measurable changes in brand equity and international perception metrics.
Supplement the Robarts (2023) news citation with official PIF reports or academic studies to ensure information reliability.
Heading âTable of Contentsâ uses a font size that is inconsistent with other section headers; adjust formatting to ensure uniform heading hierarchy.
Sentence ending abruptly (âThe contract considered Cristianoâs brand personalityâ) needs completion to clearly convey its intended argument.
Methodology justifies secondary data use but omits explicit sampling and source credibility criteria (e.g., dates, evaluation standards); include these to strengthen rigor.
Literature review clearly articulates key theoretical concepts and seminal authors, demonstrating strong subject knowledge and appropriate source application.
Consider discussing limitations of the presented frameworks (e.g., Aakerâs model) when applied to an individual athlete context to deepen critical evaluation.
Theoretical discussion lacks consideration of each modelâs applicability constraints; critically reflect on framework boundaries.
Specific claims about Vision 2030 projects require citation of official policy documents or government sources for validation.
Long, complex sentences imped readability; consider breaking them into shorter, clearer statements to improve flow.
Integrating product life cycle with brand familiarity reflects strong conceptual synthesis and coherence in framework application.
